reasoning:
A snap election held in Greenland on March 11, 2025, resulted in a victory for the center-right Demokraatit (Democrats) party, which strongly advocates for a cautious and gradual approach to independence. On March 28, 2025, a broad coalition government was formed that included four of the five parties in parliament, while intentionally excluding the ultra-nationalist Naleraq party (which had campaigned on a pledge to hold a fast independence referendum). 

The new Prime Minister, Jens-Frederik Nielsen, has explicitly stated that his government's priority is building economic foundations and that they "don't want independence tomorrow." As of early May 2025, the agreed-upon policy of the new coalition is widely recognized by experts as a cautious, long-term approach to sovereignty, which contradicts any rush to set an official date for a referendum. 

Furthermore, formally scheduling a referendum practically requires lengthy bilateral negotiations with Denmark on a separation agreement first, a process experts estimate will take years. Given that the resolution deadline is July 1, 2025—less than two months away—it is extremely unlikely that the new gradualist government will make an official announcement scheduling an independence referendum in that brief timeframe. The probability is estimated at the lowest allowable threshold of 5%.
